speaker: interviewer
text: The date is December 7th, 1995. Survivor is Kurt Klein. The interviewer is Louise Bobrow. The city is Scottsdale, in the state of Arizona. The country is United States of America, and the language is in English. My name is Louise Bobrow. Today's date is December 7th, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Kurt Klein. The interview is being conducted in Scottsdale, Arizona.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your complete name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Kurt Klein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-U-R-T K-L-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: July 2nd, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your present age?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a town by name of Walldorf, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Walldorf for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-A-L-L-D-O-R-F.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this located in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's only a few miles outside of Heidelberg, in the south of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is this the city where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe Walldorf for me? What was it like, prior to your leaving Walldorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it depends, of course, whether we're talking about prior to 1933 or-- or-- or later.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll start with earlier, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- but Walldorf was a town of 5,000 inhabitants, many of them farmers who had acreage around the town, but also a general run of merchants. And we had a small Jewish community that was well integrated into the life of the town. And there was free association with-- with people, with non-Jews. And there seemed to be-- as I was growing up, there seemed to be absolutely no-- no barriers to-- to entering the mainstream of the-- whatever society there was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the population of Jews during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, around '33, there were only 53 people. And-- which that number gradually decreased as people not only died and-- and no new ones came, but-- but also there were a number of them who-- who moved out of town. And some of the younger people, after they had a profession, would leave town to-- to go to the larger cities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many synagogues?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one synagogue. And we had sort of an all-purpose-- well, he was known as a-- as a reciter. He was not a rabbi, because the town was actually too small to support a rabbi. We could have, of course, gone to a synagogue in Heidelberg, which had a regular synagogue with rabbi and everything. But nevertheless, this was a fairly close-knit Jewish society and congregation in-- in-- in Walldorf. And the reciter also served in-- in several roles. He was a religious school teacher. He was a shochet, for ritual slaughter. He presided over, let's say, a bris. And so he was an all-around man for that job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the home where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- it was on one of the major streets in-- in that town. And it was a two-story house, as-- as so many others were there, in the back of which we had a garden, in which we grew a lot of the things, vegetables and so on, and flowers. And that helped be almost-- well, not quite self-sufficient, but it helped in general, because this was also considered the country, because it was a small town, so we had access to-- to a lot of produce that we might not have had in a city.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like you to talk now a little bit about your parents. Your father, his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father's name was Ludwig, L-U-D-W-I-G, Klein. He had six other brothers and three sisters. And he was born and grew up in the same house I was born in and grew up. And he took over my grandfather's business, which had to do with-- with hops and tobacco and-- and grain, which was being auctioned off among the farmers in that area. And-- and-- and they were brokers for those, for produce and for those products, and would sell 'em. For instance, the hops, my father would process the hops and treat them, and-- and-- and sell them to the breweries.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his personality like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he-- he was actually something of a scholarly type. He-- he he didn't show emotions too easily, but he was a very caring person. And, in fact, I-- I-- I never remember having any harsh words with him or from his side. And it-- I had a lot of respect for him. But he was more or less forced to take over this business, at a time when perhaps he should have become or gone-- become an academic or something like that. But of course, in those days, there-- there was no money for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Alice. And her maiden name was Nahm, N-A-H-M. And she-- she was very kind, with a good sense of humor, and very, very industrious, and always trying to help in every area where it was needed. She also cared a great deal for others and helped others wherever she could. And this, of course, was later to come into play even more as conditions grew worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have an occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had no occupation per se, but she would conduct some small business to help along with the family finances. Don't forget, these were very rough times between the two World Wars. Germany was economically [BACKGROUND NOISE] tremendously depressed, which affected everybody. And especially the-- the-- the unchecked, wild inflationary period in the early '20s ruined so many businesses. And, of course, my father was suffering from that, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe how some of the Jewish holidays were celebrated in your home? For example, Shabbos, did you celebrate Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- we always had a standard Shabbos dinner, you might say, which would start out with-- with fish and soup, and-- and then possibly a pot roast or something like that. And we-- you know, it was simply expected that we would always have a regular Shabbos dinner with wine, and naturally, with [NON-ENGLISH] and the usual rituals.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover we usually had in our home. But it could happen that someone else might invite us. But we-- we always gave a seder. And I remember that that-- you know, that followed the-- the-- all the rituals. And while we were not ultra-orthodox, we we-- we were observant, and were what you might consider conservative. The-- I-- I do remember certain incidents from-- from seders. For instance, it was customary that in the prayer books that we used, people had made notations, that, for instance, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on this day in the year [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1884, it snowed on Passover in April. And-- and so, we were-- this always brought back reminiscences of former times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other holidays that come to mind that you celebrated in particular, that you'd like to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember one holiday, which in this case, this doesn't apply so much to the home, but rather to the synagogue we had. [NON-ENGLISH] was being celebrated in the synagogue by bringing in-- actually bringing in young trees from the surrounding woods, putting them in clay pots along the aisles and the pews. And so there was always sort of a-- a forest inside the synagogue. That's one thing that stands out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sukkot? Did you have a sukkah in your yard, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We ourselves did not, but our neighbors did. And we availed ourselves of-- of that sukkah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. And that, of course, was a big event for-- for me. I-- I studied many months before then for that. And it also meant that the reciter, who was the head of the Jewish congregation, by name of Hahn, made the most of it, because he saw his charges dwindle to almost none. And after me, there were perhaps two more boys who became bar mitzvah in-- in Walldorf. So he made the most of it, and he really tried to instill in me all the things that were in his command. He, by the way, had a son who became a rabbi of renown in-- in the town-- in the city of Mainz. And later on, he had a pulpit in New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Hugo Hahn.

speaker: interviewer
text: What schools did you attend in Walldorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had four years of elementary school in Walldorf, after which I switched to a-- a high school, actually, a level above normal high school, in Heidelberg, where I also had four years. At that point, it was 1934, and the decrees against the Jews, and especially the economic decrees, had gotten to a point where my father found it extremely difficult to pay the tuition that it took for-- for high school in Germany. And so it was thought best that I take up a trade or do something that Jewish young people were still allowed to do, because by that time, we-- we felt some of the restrictions that were being imposed. And so I-- I always having liked books and literature and so on, thought that the closest thing to that might be to go into the printing business, which I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mention your siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, both-- both my sister and my brother were considerably older than I, by-- my sister by 9 years, and my-- my brother by 12. And so they actually left home, you know, while I was still growing up in my home. And, of course, I-- my sister was there a little longer. But my brother left home fairly early-- I almost don't remember him living under the same roof-- and went to-- to larger cities, such as, well, first, Mannheim, Stuttgart, and-- and Breslau, where he-- he was really a railroad buff, all-- even as a very young boy. And so it was quite clear for him that he would go into the travel business. And he went with one of the large steamship companies in-- in Breslau, which allowed him also to maintain-- he-- he could recite railroad schedules by heart. He knew exactly where and when a certain express train would stop at a certain station. And-- and that-- that all helped him a great deal later in his profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name was--?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Max. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- did you want me to talk about my sister? My sister's name was Irmgard. We-- for short, we called her Gertie. And she decided to go into nursing, and also left home-- oh, I forget now when, but I would think in-- in the early '30s. '34, possibly-- '33, '34-- and went to Stuttgart for nurse's training. And because she could gradually see-- well, we all became aware that this was the handwriting on the wall, there was no future, especially for young persons, Jews, in Germany. We all sort of mentally and actually prepared ourselves for emigration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never-- they were not alive. My-- my mother was actually an orphan. And and my father's parents-- well, my grandfather died 18 years before I was born. And my grandmother died at-- at the end of World War I, in 1918. When she knew that all her sons were coming home, she made a statement, well, now I can die, knowing that they are all safe. And-- and a few weeks later, and before any of them came home, she went down to the basement-- we had a basement, of course, a cellar-- and took a misstep and fell down the stairs and died, so she never got to see her sons. But she knew that they were safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe yourself, as a youngster growing up? What were you like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- [SIGHS] I was inclined to be playful. I liked books a great deal. I liked the theater and music. And I-- I was kind of carefree. And I-- I-- I had a lot of so-called friends in those days, with whom we would play endlessly. And, living in the country, there were actually areas open to us that-- that someone growing up in a city would-- would never have. We had these woods. We would take long hikes. We would explore the woods and-- and the mountains, and do hiking and swimming. And I also liked the movies a great deal. And-- and every week, at least once, I-- I-- I would go to the weekend movie that was being shown in those days. And, in that sense, I also learned a great deal about America that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember an American movie you saw that you liked?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I'm trying. For instance, I-- I know-- one of the things that I was absolutely wild about, and so were my friends in school, were westerns. And-- and there was, of course, Tom Mix was a big star in-- in-- in those days, with his-- I think his white horse by name of Trigger, if I'm not mistaken. And-- and we couldn't get enough of that. And we played that out to the ultimate. And this was also reinforced by a series of-- of western stories written by a German author by name of Karl May, who wrote some very definitive works on-- on the American West and on the Indians and the plight of the Indians.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your dreams, or ambitions of the future, as a young man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, somehow, through that, I always thought that somehow or other I would wind up in-- in America, and presumably in the west, where I now live. [LAUGHS] I-- in that sense, it's-- it's quite funny that I should wind up here in Arizona.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many non-Jewish friends growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. More than Jewish, simply because there weren't any. But I-- but I have to qualify that. First of all, once Hitler came to power, of course, they-- they fell by the wayside, the non-Jewish friends. And I-- I will tell you something about that in a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we concentrated on-- on the few Jewish in my case boys who were available. There were two in the town where I lived. But once I went to school in Heidelberg, I-- I did have quite a number of Jewish classmates, and also belonged to an organization, similar to Scouts, where we would meet on weekends, and discuss anything of interest, whether it be literature, politics, or the movies, anything at all. And we-- we would go for hikes in the mountains of Heidelberg, and also take sometimes two- three-day backpack trips, and some of them even longer, staying in youth hostels. And this we considered a great deal of fun.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we also played some sports, volleyball and-- and things of that nature. And there was a very easy exchange and banter. And-- and I remember those times very fondly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you personally experience any anti-Semitism before the rise of Hitler? Did anything happen to you personally that you can recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never before, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Hitler's rise to power in 1933 begin to change your life? Could you start talking about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, we had seen it coming. I grew up in an era of almost total political strife after World War I. And I-- I saw this monster of the Nazi party grow and grow. And no matter what any of us thought could be done about it, they were not to be stopped, although they had their own setbacks within the progression of that. But in-- in the end, we could see that the monster was getting bigger and bigger. And there were, of course, many manifestations of anti-Semitism caused by them. For instance, I remember visiting in one town, where they-- surrounded by mountains-- where they had one huge rock, which was the mountain. And one night, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Nazis climbed up that-- that rock. And in the morning, there was a huge-- I can't even tell you how large-- swastika. And, of course, everywhere, there were-- there were their Nazi slogans to be found, on-- sometimes on walls of houses, on barns, on wherever. And many of those slogans dealt, you know, with Jews, and-- and what a low form of existence Jews were, I mean, in the-- in the most blatant manner. So we-- we saw what was coming. But of course, many Jews deluded themselves, saying, we have lived here all our lives, for generations, in fact. We fought in World War I and some of us died, and they will never do anything really drastic to us. But I personally was not quite that optimistic in the time immediately before Hitler's rise to power. And after that, of course, we were to find out that each day or each week brought new decrees that made our life harder.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of those new decrees, new restrictions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In March of 1933-- Hitler came to power in January of '33. I came home from school one time, and- and I see that there are two stormtroopers, SA men, standing guard in front of our house. And I wasn't quite sure whether they would let me through, but they apparently knew me, and did let me through. But this was the day in which the Nazis began their infamous boycott of Jewish homes and businesses. It was-- it was geared to keep the populace from doing business with or being friendly with Jews. And that-- that was their overt demonstration of-- of how they would keep the people from associating with Jews. And from then on in, things got progressively worse. There were, of course, the Nuremberg Laws, which followed in '35, and-- in-- in which Jews were removed from all official positions, anything to do with schools, universities, the medical establishment insofar as it was municipal. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so there were all sorts of restrictions like that, where people couldn't keep their jobs. And also, businesses were already feeling the pinch, Jewish businesses, because the boycott had its effect, of course, in that non-Jews didn't-- bought-- bought from Jews in-- in lesser numbers, although it didn't completely dry up yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect your father's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was effectively cut out of-- of many things, such as he had to acquire his hops at auctions. And at those auctions, whereas before he would have been just one of the participants, even though he still could go to them, they made sure that somebody else would outbid him and he couldn't do it. He couldn't obtain the merchandise, so to speak. And this became harder and harder. And my parents found ways of substituting some other produce where it wasn't possible with hops anymore for a while. And in season, they could ship asparagus, for which this area was renowned, to other parts of Germany and-- and to people who-- who-- who wanted asparagus from that area, and-- or grain, on a very small scale. And my-- my mother found a few items, such as special types of coffee and chocolate, that she could sell to friends and-- and relatives in town and-- and in other cities.
